project_overview.project_development_objective: To establish an efficient and accessible land administration system
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 1 million people, including 250,000-500,000 in Freetown and 250,000-500,000 in provinces; government institutions; private sector surveyors and notaries

geographic_scope.coverage_type: National with focus on Freetown and selected provincial areas
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - Sierra Leone is post-conflict (civil war 1991-2002) with remaining fragility risks. Land-related disputes played prominent role in civil war. Large-scale land acquisitions and unresolved land issues pose significant risk to social cohesion. Project addresses fragility through land tenure security and institutional strengthening.

key_components.description: Strengthen legal and institutional framework through institutional development (office buildings, equipment, training programs), legal reform (updating land laws and regulations), and sensitization campaigns focusing on women's and youths' land rights

Establish modern digital Land Information System combining registry and cadastre, digitize paper archives, develop base maps and geodetic network with CORS stations, enable sex-disaggregated data collection

key_components.component_name: Recording and Registration of Land Tenure Rights
key_components.description: Improve land registration processes, pilot and roll-out systematic registration in Freetown and customary areas in provinces, establish local land committees with 30% female membership, ensure women's land rights registration
Finance Project Coordination Unit operations, monitoring and evaluation, studies, customer feedback surveys, impact evaluation, grievance redress mechanism establishment and operation
Allows reallocation of uncommitted funds to support emergency response and reconstruction following eligible crisis or emergency

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Lands, Housing and Country Planning (MLHCP)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (chaired by Ministry of Finance, includes MLHCP and OARG); Project Technical Committee (quarterly meetings with representatives from 13+ government ministries and agencies, civil society); VGGT Technical Working Group; Inter-Ministerial Task Force; MLHCP-OARG Working Group; Civil Society Organizations participation
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF; Sierra Leone National Public Procurement Act 2016; use of STEP system; prior and post-review procedures; aggregation of packages; private sector sensitization; standard procurement documents
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: M&E Specialist in PCU; quarterly and annual monitoring reports; customer satisfaction surveys (year 1, mid-term, close); Grievance Redress Mechanism with social safeguards specialist; impact evaluation in collaboration with Gender Innovation Lab; indicator tracking through LIS once functional

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertainty regarding passage of Customary Land Rights Bill and National Land Commission Bill, though both have been submitted to Parliament. Project does not depend on passage but implementation would be more efficient with new laws.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design allows implementation under current legal framework if needed; ongoing Development Policy Operation includes prior action on NLCB Board appointment; close monitoring of legislative progress; project design adaptability

identified_risks.risk_description: Low capacity within MLHCP and other public sector entities. This is MLHCP's first World Bank project. Staff lack training since 2016. Surveyor Training School defunct since 2009. Concerns about land registration functions transfer from OARG to proposed Land Commission.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Dedicated Component 1 for capacity building; comprehensive training programs (short-term, long-term, on-the-job, internships); coordination with universities and regional networks; MLHCP-OARG working group established; incremental approach to implementation; regular World Bank supervision

identified_risks.risk_description: MLHCP has no prior World Bank project experience. Limited FM capacity. Weak PFM environment in country. Potential for fraud, corruption, collusion, and weak internal controls.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruitment of qualified FM Specialist and Assistant; FM training for PCU staff; centralized FM functions in PCU; designated US$ account; report-based disbursements; quarterly IFRs; annual audited financial statements; internal audit; anti-corruption guidelines; regular FM supervision (at least twice yearly)

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited procurement capacity; insufficient knowledge of World Bank Procurement Regulations and STEP system; inefficiencies and delays; insufficient competition; weak complaint redress system; fraud and corruption risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruitment of qualified Procurement Specialist and Assistant; regular procurement clinics and training; capacity building on STEP; aggregation of small packages; private sector sensitization; biannual complaint reporting; disclosure of procurement plans and awards; 20% post-review sample initially; monitoring through STEP

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risks from land registration activities including conflicting property boundaries, overlapping rights, boundary contestations between communities, encroachments on land use boundaries. Potential land acquisition and involuntary displacement for office construction. SEA/SH risks from construction work and land registration activities. Asbestos exposure if demolition required.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ESMF, RPF, LMP, SEP prepared and disclosed; site-specific ESMPs to be prepared; Asbestos Management Plan if demolition needed; GRM and ADR processes; participatory survey and community consultation; three E&S officers in PCU; gender quotas in land committees; GBV Action Plan; SEA/SH risk mitigation in contractor codes of conduct; climate-resilient building design; prioritize new construction over demolition

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of new land laws not being passed by Parliament, affecting project implementation efficiency. Outdated legal framework from 1960s not aligned with modern land administration or gender equality principles.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design allows operation under current legal framework; Component 1 supports legal reform; World Bank monitoring of legislative progress; Development Policy Operation includes land law prior action; incremental legal reform approach

identified_risks.risk_description: Post-conflict context with remaining fragility. Land disputes historically contributed to civil war. Large-scale land acquisitions ignoring local rights fuel fragility risks. Potential for elite resistance to reforms and distrust between government and customary institutions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stakeholder engagement and consultation throughout; public sensitization campaigns; CSO involvement in design and implementation; VGGT principles integration; participatory processes for land registration; GRM for dispute resolution; ADR mechanisms; coordination with customary authorities; focus on women's and vulnerable groups' rights

identified_risks.risk_description: Complex project with multiple components and stakeholders. First large-scale land sector project for MLHCP. Challenges in integrating OARG and MLHCP systems. LIS development complexity. Customary land registration complexity with large family units.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design informed by lessons from similar projects (Liberia, Cote d'Ivoire, Senegal); incremental approach to LIS development; business process re-engineering study; pilot activities before scale-up; fit-for-purpose surveying approaches; assessment of previous UNFAO pilots; dedicated technical experts in World Bank team; regular technical missions

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1 - Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS3 - Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS5 - Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS6 - Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS10 - Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level GRM to be established within 60 days of effectiveness. Will record, track, and resolve grievances from beneficiaries, citizens, specialized users (surveyors, notaries), and government agencies. Social safeguards specialist in PCU will respond to feedback. GRM will serve as feedback and learning mechanism. Results reported in quarterly and annual reports. Also accessible to World Bank's corporate GRS and Inspection Panel.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Strong gender focus throughout project. Addresses gender gaps: women's land rights registered less often than men (20% urban women vs 28% urban men); lack of gender-disaggregated land data; lack of women's representation in land decision-making bodies. Project will: register women's land rights individually, jointly, or as families; create LIS functions for sex-disaggregated data; establish local land committees with minimum 30% female membership; ensure women's participation in all land registration steps; provide gender-focused sensitization campaigns; support legal reforms strengthening women's rights; conduct gender assessment and develop GBV Action Plan; coordinate with UN Women on drone mapping for women's rights; target 150 female land sector professionals trained
vulnerable_groups: Women (particularly regarding land ownership and inheritance rights)
Poorest segments of population (informal land users, cannot afford registration fees)
People living in high-risk climate zones
Marginalized groups under customary tenure systems

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly addresses conflict risks through: (1) Land tenure security - unresolved land issues and gender inequality in land sector pose significant risk to social cohesion; 60% of High Court cases arise from land disputes; (2) Dispute resolution - GRM and ADR mechanisms to resolve land-related conflicts; (3) Inclusive processes - participatory survey and registration with community consultation to prevent boundary disputes; (4) Customary institution engagement - coordination with Paramount Chiefs and Chiefdom Councils; (5) Women's empowerment - addressing gender discrimination in land access reduces conflict drivers; (6) Transparency - digitized system reduces corruption and disputes; (7) Stakeholder coordination - VGGT TWG, Inter-Ministerial Task Force, CSO involvement; (8) Sensitization - public awareness campaigns on land rights and processes
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sierra Leone remains fragile post-conflict state (civil war 1991-2002). Key fragility factors: (1) Land-related disputes played prominent role in civil war; (2) Large-scale land acquisitions ignoring local rights fuel fragility; (3) Weak institutions and governance; (4) Low institutional capacity; (5) Limited access to basic infrastructure; (6) Poor governance measures (effectiveness, regulatory quality, corruption, rule of law); (7) Difficult business environment hindering economic diversification and job creation; (8) Climate vulnerability (second most intensive rainfalls in Africa, sea level rise, coastal erosion, deforestation); (9) High poverty (71% rural, 46.3% other urban, 29.1% Freetown); (10) Limited structural economic change; (11) Weak PFM environment. Project addresses fragility through institutional strengthening, legal reform, tenure security, and inclusive governance.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks include: (1) Potential for elite resistance to land reforms threatening status quo; (2) Distrust between government and customary institutions; (3) SEA/SH risks from construction work and land registration activities with vulnerable populations; (4) Occupational health and safety risks during construction; (5) Asbestos exposure risks if demolition required. Mitigation: GRM and ADR for dispute resolution; participatory processes; CSO involvement; GBV Action Plan; contractor codes of conduct; worker training; community sensitization; Asbestos Management Plan; climate-resilient building design; occupational health and safety measures
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Potential displacement risks identified: (1) Land acquisition for office construction in Freetown and provincial capitals may require acquisition of private land or displacement of squatters; (2) Involuntary resettlement possible if project not carried out on public property; (3) Climate-induced displacement - project beneficiaries affected by flooding, mudslides, coastal erosion, sea level rise; (4) Potential population migration due to climate-induced land tenure changes. Mitigation: RPF prepared detailing land acquisition processes; site-specific RAPs to be prepared if needed; compensation at full replacement cost before civil works; preference for government-owned land and new construction; project will not register land in high-risk climate zones to avoid incentivizing settlement; risk mapping to identify vulnerable populations; coordination with NDMA for disaster response
